title: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain
rawPassageText:
READING PASSAGE 2
You should spend about 20 minutes on Questions 14-26, which are based on Reading Passage 2 below.

Music and the Brain

A

For much of the twentieth century, music was often treated by scientists as a pleasant cultural extra rather than a serious route into the workings of the brain. That view has changed rapidly. Brain-imaging studies now suggest that listening to a song, keeping time with a beat or learning an instrument involves networks concerned with hearing, movement, memory, emotion and attention. Researchers are beginning to appreciate that music is not simply entertainment: it is a demanding mental activity which can reveal how different brain systems cooperate. This does not surprise musicians, who have long known that a performance depends on prediction, physical coordination and feeling, all operating at once.

B

The process begins with sound waves reaching the inner ear, where they are converted into electrical signals and sent to the auditory cortex. From there, the brain does more than register pitch and volume. It searches for patterns, groups notes into phrases and forms predictions about what is likely to happen next. If a drumbeat is regular, listeners often move before they consciously decide to do so, because rhythm also activates motor areas of the brain. These links between hearing and movement help explain why walking, clapping and dancing can become synchronised with music so easily.

C

Music also engages the reward system, a set of circuits involved in motivation and pleasure. When a listener expects a favourite chorus and then hears it arrive, the brain may release dopamine, a chemical associated with anticipation and satisfaction. This is one reason why a familiar song can feel both predictable and exciting. Neuroscientists are especially interested in the short pause before a musical resolution, because the listener is suspended between certainty and surprise. According to several researchers, this moment shows how the brain turns expectation into emotion.

D

The connection between music and movement has encouraged its use in rehabilitation. Some stroke patients who cannot easily produce ordinary speech can sing short phrases with fewer difficulties, because melody may recruit right-hemisphere regions that are less damaged. Therapists sometimes use a technique in which patients tap a rhythm while singing simple sentences, gradually moving from melody back towards normal speech. Clinicians stress, however, that patients must continue to benefit from the process: the songs should be chosen with them, the exercises should match their energy levels, and musical tasks should not become another source of frustration.

E

Memory provides another reason for studying music. People who cannot recall a recent conversation may still recognise songs from adolescence, and familiar music can sometimes bring back memories linked to people, places or routines. In dementia care, carefully selected recordings are therefore used to support attention and social contact. The effect is not a cure, and it does not occur in every case, but it can be striking. A person who has remained silent during a group activity may suddenly sing a line from an old song, giving carers a brief but valuable route into communication.

F

Music is also being tested in hospitals as a way of reducing pain and anxiety. In one trial involving patients before minor surgery, about 60 percent of those offered a choice of calming tracks reported lower discomfort than patients who rested in silence. Researchers suggest several possible explanations: music can distract attention from pain, slow breathing and reduce cortisol, a hormone released during stress. Yet the details matter. A piece that relaxes one listener may irritate another, and music that is too loud or too unfamiliar may increase tension rather than reduce it.

G

Outside clinics, scientists are studying how music affects concentration. Many people use background music while studying or working, believing that it improves their mood and keeps them on task. The evidence is mixed. Simple or repetitive tasks may become more pleasant with music, but complex work that depends on language or calculation can suffer when lyrics or changing rhythms compete for attention. Dr Elena Varga, a cognitive psychologist, argues that listeners often misjudge this effect: background playlists may make work feel easier while quietly reducing accuracy.

H

The cultural significance of music makes the subject difficult to reduce to brain activity alone. Lullabies, hymns, national songs and protest music do more than stimulate neurons; they carry identity, memory and social belonging. Dr Sofia Alvarez, a music therapist, warns that therapy loses power when it strips those histories away. Professor Malik Rahman, who studies neuroimaging, makes a similar point from another angle: scanner images can show timing and blood flow, but not the whole meaning of a song in someone's life. For Dr Thomas Greene, the practical lesson is that music-based treatment must be simple enough for families, teachers and community workers to repeat outside specialist settings.

Questions 14-17

Reading Passage 2 has eight paragraphs, A-H.

Which paragraph contains the following information?

Write the correct letter, A-H, in boxes 14-17 on your answer sheet.

14 reference to the need to ensure that patients using music therapy continue to gain from it
15 reference to the idea that researchers are beginning to recognise the value of music as a way to understand the brain
16 the proportion of patients in a study who experienced less discomfort when music was used
17 mention of the cultural significance of music

Questions 18-22

Complete the sentences below.

Choose ONE WORD ONLY from the passage for each answer.

Write your answers in boxes 18-22 on your answer sheet.

18 When listening to music, the brain forms .................... about what it may hear next.
19 Music can activate the brain's .................... system, which is connected with motivation and pleasure.
20 Some stroke patients may use singing as a step towards recovering ordinary .................... .
21 Familiar songs can sometimes help people with dementia bring back .................... .
22 Calming music may lower levels of ...................., a hormone connected with stress.

Questions 23-26

Look at the following statements (Questions 23-26) and the list of experts below.

Match each statement with the correct expert, A-D.

Write the correct letter, A-D, in boxes 23-26 on your answer sheet.

23 People may overestimate how helpful music is when they are doing demanding work.
24 Music therapy should take account of the listener's personal and social past.
25 Scientific images of the brain cannot fully explain the experience of music.
26 Music-based treatment should be easy to continue beyond professional clinics.

List of Experts

A Dr Sofia Alvarez
B Professor Malik Rahman
C Dr Elena Varga
D Dr Thomas Greene
rawAnswerKey:
Question 14: reference to the need to ensure that patients using music therapy continue to gain from it

Correct Answer: D

Accepted Answers: D

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: Clinicians stress, however, that patients must continue to benefit from the process

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ D เพราะโจทย์ถามถึงความจำเป็นที่ผู้ป่วยซึ่งใช้ music therapy ต้อง continue to gain from it หรือยังได้รับประโยชน์ต่อไป ย่อหน้า D พูดเรื่อง rehabilitation ของ stroke patients และระบุชัดว่า clinicians stress that patients must continue to benefit from the process ข้อความนี้ตรงกับโจทย์ทั้งเรื่อง patients, music therapy process และ continue to benefit จึงต้องเลือกย่อหน้า D ไม่ใช่ย่อหน้าอื่นที่พูดเรื่องสมองหรือความจำ

Paraphrased Vocabulary: continue to gain from it = continue to benefit from the process; patients using music therapy = patients in rehabilitation using songs and rhythm; need to ensure = clinicians stress; answer signal = D

Question 15: reference to the idea that researchers are beginning to recognise the value of music as a way to understand the brain

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: Researchers are beginning to appreciate that music is not simply entertainment: it is a demanding mental activity which can reveal how different brain systems cooperate.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะโจทย์ถามแนวคิดว่า researchers เริ่ม recognise value ของ music เพื่อเข้าใจ brain ย่อหน้า A ระบุว่า researchers are beginning to appreciate ว่า music ไม่ใช่แค่ entertainment แต่เป็น demanding mental activity ที่ reveal how different brain systems cooperate ดังนั้น recognise the value เทียบกับ beginning to appreciate และ understand the brain เทียบกับ reveal brain systems cooperate ได้ตรงที่สุด

Paraphrased Vocabulary: beginning to recognise = beginning to appreciate; value of music = not simply entertainment / demanding mental activity; understand the brain = reveal how brain systems cooperate; answer signal = A

Question 16: the proportion of patients in a study who experienced less discomfort when music was used

Correct Answer: F

Accepted Answers: F

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: about 60 percent of those offered a choice of calming tracks reported lower discomfort

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ F เพราะโจทย์ถาม proportion ของผู้ป่วยใน study ที่รู้สึก discomfort ลดลงเมื่อใช้ music ย่อหน้า F กล่าวถึง hospital trial ก่อน minor surgery และระบุสัดส่วนว่า about 60 percent reported lower discomfort เมื่อได้เลือก calming tracks คำว่า proportion ในโจทย์จึงตรงกับ about 60 percent และ experienced less discomfort ตรงกับ reported lower discomfort ชัดเจน

Paraphrased Vocabulary: proportion = about 60 percent; experienced less discomfort = reported lower discomfort; music was used = offered calming tracks; answer signal = F

Question 17: mention of the cultural significance of music

Correct Answer: H

Accepted Answers: H

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: The cultural significance of music makes the subject difficult to reduce to brain activity alone.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ H เพราะโจทย์ถามการ mention cultural significance of music และย่อหน้า H ใช้วลีนี้ตรงตัวว่า The cultural significance of music นอกจากนี้ย่อหน้ายังขยายความด้วยตัวอย่าง lullabies, hymns, national songs และ protest music ที่เกี่ยวข้องกับ identity, memory และ social belonging จึงเป็นย่อหน้าที่พูดถึงความหมายทางวัฒนธรรมของดนตรีโดยตรง

Paraphrased Vocabulary: cultural significance = identity, memory and social belonging; mention = states directly; music = lullabies, hymns, national songs and protest music; answer signal = H

Question 18: When listening to music, the brain forms .................... about what it may hear next.

Correct Answer: predictions

Accepted Answers: predictions

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: forms predictions about what is likely to happen next

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ predictions เพราะ summary ถามว่า brain forms ____ about what it may hear next และ passage ระบุว่า the brain forms predictions about what is likely to happen next โดยตรง คำว่า may hear next ในโจทย์เป็นการ paraphrase จาก likely to happen next ส่วนคำที่เป็นคำนามหลัง forms คือ predictions จึงต้องตอบคำนี้เท่านั้นตามคำสั่ง ONE WORD ONLY

Paraphrased Vocabulary: what it may hear next = what is likely to happen next; forms = forms; listening to music = process begins with sound / brain searches for patterns; answer signal = predictions

Question 19: Music can activate the brain's .................... system, which is connected with motivation and pleasure.

Correct Answer: reward

Accepted Answers: reward

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: Music also engages the reward system, a set of circuits involved in motivation and pleasure.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ reward เพราะโจทย์บอกว่า music can activate the brain's ____ system และ passage ใช้วลีว่า Music also engages the reward system โดยอธิบายต่อว่าเป็น circuits involved in motivation and pleasure คำว่า activate ในโจทย์เทียบกับ engages ใน passage ได้ และ connected with motivation and pleasure เทียบกับ involved in motivation and pleasure ได้ตรงกัน

Paraphrased Vocabulary: activate = engages; connected with = involved in; motivation and pleasure = motivation and pleasure; answer signal = reward

Question 20: Some stroke patients may use singing as a step towards recovering ordinary .................... .

Correct Answer: speech

Accepted Answers: speech

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: gradually moving from melody back towards normal speech

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ speech เพราะโจทย์ถามว่า stroke patients ใช้ singing เป็นขั้นตอนเพื่อกลับไปสู่ ordinary ____ และ passage ระบุว่า therapists ใช้วิธีให้ผู้ป่วยร้อง simple sentences แล้ว gradually moving from melody back towards normal speech คำว่า ordinary ในโจทย์เทียบกับ normal ใน passage ได้ ดังนั้นคำที่เติมคือ speech ซึ่งเป็นคำเดียวจาก passage

Paraphrased Vocabulary: ordinary = normal; recovering = moving back towards; singing = melody / singing simple sentences; answer signal = speech

Question 21: Familiar songs can sometimes help people with dementia bring back .................... .

Correct Answer: memories

Accepted Answers: memories

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: familiar music can sometimes bring back memories

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ memories เพราะโจทย์บอกว่า familiar songs can sometimes help people with dementia bring back ____ และ passage ระบุว่า familiar music can sometimes bring back memories ในย่อหน้าที่พูดถึง dementia care คำว่า songs ในโจทย์เทียบกับ music ใน passage ได้ และ bring back ใช้ตรงกัน จึงต้องเติม memories ตามคำใน passage

Paraphrased Vocabulary: familiar songs = familiar music; help people with dementia = dementia care / people who cannot recall recent conversation; bring back = bring back; answer signal = memories

Question 22: Calming music may lower levels of ...................., a hormone connected with stress.

Correct Answer: cortisol

Accepted Answers: cortisol

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: reduce cortisol, a hormone released during stress

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ cortisol เพราะโจทย์ถามว่า calming music may lower levels of ____ ซึ่งเป็น hormone connected with stress ย่อหน้า F ระบุว่า music can reduce cortisol, a hormone released during stress คำว่า lower levels เทียบกับ reduce ได้ และ connected with stress เทียบกับ released during stress ได้ตรงกัน ดังนั้นคำตอบคือ cortisol

Paraphrased Vocabulary: lower levels = reduce; connected with stress = released during stress; calming music = calming tracks; answer signal = cortisol

Question 23: People may overestimate how helpful music is when they are doing demanding work.

Correct Answer: C

Accepted Answers: C

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: Dr Elena Varga, a cognitive psychologist, argues that listeners often misjudge this effect: background playlists may make work feel easier while quietly reducing accuracy.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ C เพราะ statement บอกว่าคน may overestimate ว่าดนตรีช่วยตอนทำ demanding work มากแค่ไหน ย่อหน้า G ระบุว่า Dr Elena Varga argues that listeners often misjudge this effect และ background playlists may make work feel easier while reducing accuracy นั่นหมายความว่าผู้ฟังอาจคิดว่าดนตรีช่วย แต่จริง ๆ งานที่ต้องใช้ภาษา/การคำนวณอาจแย่ลง ผู้เชี่ยวชาญคือ Dr Elena Varga ตัวเลือก C

Paraphrased Vocabulary: overestimate = misjudge this effect; helpful = make work feel easier; demanding work = complex work depending on language or calculation; answer signal = C

Question 24: Music therapy should take account of the listener's personal and social past.

Correct Answer: A

Accepted Answers: A

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: Dr Sofia Alvarez, a music therapist, warns that therapy loses power when it strips those histories away.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ A เพราะ statement บอกว่า music therapy ควรคำนึงถึง personal and social past ของผู้ฟัง ย่อหน้า H ระบุว่า Dr Sofia Alvarez warns that therapy loses power when it strips those histories away โดย those histories อ้างกลับไปที่ identity, memory and social belonging จากประโยคก่อนหน้า จึงตรงกับ listener's personal and social past ชัดเจน ผู้เชี่ยวชาญคือ Dr Sofia Alvarez ตัวเลือก A

Paraphrased Vocabulary: take account of = not strip away; personal and social past = identity, memory, social belonging / those histories; listener = someone's life; answer signal = A

Question 25: Scientific images of the brain cannot fully explain the experience of music.

Correct Answer: B

Accepted Answers: B

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: Professor Malik Rahman, who studies neuroimaging, makes a similar point from another angle: scanner images can show timing and blood flow, but not the whole meaning of a song in someone's life.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ B เพราะ statement พูดว่า scientific images of the brain cannot fully explain music experience ย่อหน้า H กล่าวถึง Professor Malik Rahman ผู้ศึกษา neuroimaging และบอกว่า scanner images แสดง timing and blood flow ได้ แต่ไม่ใช่ whole meaning of a song in someone's life คำว่า cannot fully explain ตรงกับ not the whole meaning ดังนั้นผู้พูดคือ Professor Malik Rahman ตัวเลือก B

Paraphrased Vocabulary: scientific images = scanner images / neuroimaging; cannot fully explain = not the whole meaning; experience of music = meaning of a song in someone's life; answer signal = B

Question 26: Music-based treatment should be easy to continue beyond professional clinics.

Correct Answer: D

Accepted Answers: D

Answer Group: IELTS Academic Reading Jan 2026 Passage 2 - Music and the Brain

Exact Portion: For Dr Thomas Greene, the practical lesson is that music-based treatment must be simple enough for families, teachers and community workers to repeat outside specialist settings.

Short Thai Explanation: คำตอบคือ D เพราะ statement บอกว่า music-based treatment ควรง่ายพอที่จะ continue beyond professional clinics ย่อหน้า H ระบุว่า Dr Thomas Greene เห็นว่า treatment must be simple enough for families, teachers and community workers to repeat outside specialist settings คำว่า beyond professional clinics ในโจทย์เทียบกับ outside specialist settings ได้ ดังนั้นผู้พูดคือ Dr Thomas Greene ตัวเลือก D

Paraphrased Vocabulary: easy to continue = simple enough to repeat; beyond professional clinics = outside specialist settings; music-based treatment = music-based treatment; answer signal = D
